mappings.rationale: Section A.1 covers ancient philosophical schools including Pythagoreanism (A.1.a), the Sophists (A.1.b), Eleaticism (A.1.c), and Atomism (A.1.d).
Section A.1.e covers Platonism as a major philosophical school in antiquity, and B.1 discusses Platonism as a metaphysical theory.
Section A.1.f covers Aristotelianism as a philosophical school, and B.1 discusses it as a metaphysical theory.
Section A.1.g covers Stoicism as a major philosophical school in antiquity.
Section A.1.h covers Epicureanism as a major philosophical school in antiquity.
Section A.1.i covers Skepticism as a philosophical school in antiquity, and C.2 discusses it as a theory of validity of knowledge.
Section A.1.j covers Scholasticism as a philosophical school, and B.1 lists Thomism as a type of metaphysical theory.
Section A.2.a covers Cartesianism as a philosophical school in the modern period, and B.1 lists it as a metaphysical theory.
Section A.2.b covers Empiricism as a philosophical school, to which Locke is a foundational contributor.
Hume is a central figure in Empiricism (A.2.b) and Skepticism, both major philosophical schools covered in this section.
Section A.2.c covers Rationalism, to which Spinoza is a central contributor with his metaphysical system.
Section A.2.c covers Rationalism, to which Leibniz is a central contributor with his philosophical system.
Section A.2.e covers Kantianism as a major philosophical school in the modern period.
Section A.2.g covers Hegelianism as a philosophical school, and A.2.f covers Idealism to which Hegel is central.
Section A.2.j covers Marxism as a philosophical school, and B.1 lists dialectical Materialism as a metaphysical theory.
Section A.2.h covers Utilitarianism as a major philosophical school in the modern period.
Mill is a central figure in Utilitarianism (A.2.h) and Empiricism (A.2.b), both major philosophical schools.
Section A.2.n covers Existentialism as a major philosophical school, and B.3 discusses it as a conception of man.
Section A.2.o covers Analytic and Linguistic philosophy as a major modern philosophical school.
Continental philosophy encompasses several schools covered here including Phenomenology (A.2.m), Existentialism (A.2.n), and Marxism (A.2.j).
Postmodernism relates to recent developments beyond the schools listed, challenging Realism (A.2.k) and other traditional positions.
Poststructuralism extends beyond Phenomenology (A.2.m) and Linguistic philosophy (A.2.o), representing a later development in philosophical schools.
Critical theory emerged from Marxism (A.2.j) and engages with multiple philosophical schools and doctrines covered in this section.
Hobbes is relevant to Materialism (A.2.d) and Empiricism (A.2.b), and his political philosophy connects to theories of conduct (D).
Kierkegaard is a foundational figure for Existentialism (A.2.n) and challenges to Rationalism.
Nietzsche challenges multiple philosophical schools and relates to theories of conduct (D) and conceptions of man (B.3).
Schopenhauer's philosophy of will relates to Idealism (A.2.f) and contributes to the reaction against Rationalism (A.2.c).
Heidegger's thought relates to Phenomenology (A.2.m) and Existentialism (A.2.n) as major philosophical schools.
Wittgenstein is central to Analytic and Linguistic philosophy (A.2.o) and Positivism/Logical Empiricism (A.2.i).
Russell is a foundational figure in Analytic philosophy (A.2.o) and contributed to Realism (A.2.k) and Logical Empiricism (A.2.i).
Foucault's work engages with multiple philosophical traditions including Marxism (A.2.j) and Phenomenology (A.2.m).
Derrida's deconstruction engages with Phenomenology (A.2.m), challenging established philosophical schools and doctrines.
Habermas engages with Marxism (A.2.j), Pragmatism (A.2.l), and Positivism (A.2.i) in developing critical social theory.
Adorno's critical theory engages with Marxism (A.2.j), Idealism (A.2.f), and Positivism (A.2.i).
German philosophy spans Rationalism (A.2.c), Kantianism (A.2.e), Idealism (A.2.f), Hegelianism (A.2.g), and Phenomenology (A.2.m).
French philosophy encompasses Cartesianism (A.2.a), Existentialism (A.2.n), Positivism (A.2.i), and Phenomenology (A.2.m).
Section B.2 covers different views on God including atheism as a position on the existence of God.
Section B.2 covers agnosticism as a view concerning the existence and knowledge of God.
Section B.2 covers humanism as a view concerning God, and B.3 lists it as a conception of man.
Section B.2 covers theism, Deism, fideism, and pantheism as views concerning God's existence and attributes.
Rousseau relates to theories of conduct (D) and the Enlightenment critique of Rationalism (A.2.c).
Augustine is relevant to Platonism (A.1.e) and Scholasticism (A.1.j), and B.1 lists his metaphysical contributions.
Objectivity relates to multiple epistemological positions including Realism (C.1), Kantianism (C.2), and Positivism (C.2).
Section C covers theories of thought and knowledge including different conceptions of objects, validity, and sources of knowledge.
Section C.1 covers different conceptions of the object of knowledge including Phenomenalism, Idealism, and Realism.
Section B.1 covers different types of metaphysical theory from Platonism through Organismic dynamism.
Free will connects to theories of conduct (D) and the mind-body problem (B.4), central philosophical doctrines.
Section B.4 covers different views on mind and body including Materialism, dualism, and immaterialism.
Arendt's thought engages with Existentialism (A.2.n), Pragmatism (A.2.l), and political philosophy doctrines.
Weil's philosophy engages with Platonism (A.1.e), mysticism, and ethical doctrines (D) across multiple traditions.
